content.problemStatement:
Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum are playing a fierce match of binary Nim. This novel game is played with NNN stacks, each of them containing only 111-s and 000-s.

Just like in normal Nim, Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum alternate turns; in their turn, a player must choose one non-empty stack and remove a positive number of elements from the top of this stack. However, Tweedle-Dee may only choose a stack with 000 at the top (and remove elements from it afterwards), and similarly, Tweedle-Dum may only choose a stack with 111 at the top. The player that cannot make a move for the first time wins.

Ada does not want to wait for the end of the game, so she asked you to determine the winner of the game if you are given the starting player. Remember that Tweedle-Dee and Tweedle-Dum are legendary grandmasters of combinatorial games, so they both always play optimally.
constraints: each character in B is either '1' or '0'
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains an integer N, followed by a space and a string S denoting the player that starts the game. Each of the following N lines contains a single binary string B representing a stack; the first character of B corresponds to the top element of the stack and the last character to the bottom element.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string "Dee" if Tweedle-Dee wins the match or "Dum" if Tweedle-Dum wins.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The Nim sum of the number of 1s and 0s in each stack is 0. Therefore, the current player will lose.
The Nim sum of the number of 1s and 0s in each stack is not 0. Therefore, the current player will win.
The Nim sum of the number of 1s and 0s in each stack is not 0. Therefore, the current player will lose.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of Nim theory. We need to calculate the Nim sum of the number of 1s and 0s in each stack. If the Nim sum is 0, then the current player will lose. Otherwise, the current player will win. We can use a loop to iterate over each test case and calculate the Nim sum for each stack.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * |B|) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of stacks, and |B| is the maximum length of a binary string.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * |B|) — where N is the number of stacks and |B| is the maximum length of a binary string.
